summary: I was born and raised in Chicago, Illinois, living at 6231 Stewart Avenue with my parents before I was drafted. It was there that I finished high school and started studying to be a linotype operator while working at Mercury Typesetting. Chicago was my home base before I entered the Army in April 1943.

Before I was drafted, I worked as an apprentice at Mercury Typesetting in Chicago. I was studying to be a linotype operator while employed there, having just finished high school. It was a young start for me, around 18 or 19 years old, before I entered the Army.

I was drafted into the United States Army on April 5th, 1943, choosing it partly because my father had served in World War I. I trained as an infantry replacement at Camp Wheeler, Georgia, and served overseas in the Pacific Theater. The Army was a defining part of my young adult life, shaping my experiences through combat and camaraderie.

My father served in the United States Army during World War I, and that influenced my decision to join the Army when I was drafted in 1943. I wanted to follow in his footsteps and serve my country as he did. Though I didn't experience World War I myself, his service was a big part of my family's history.

I was inducted into the Army at Camp Grant, Illinois, where I first met many other young men from all over the country. It was an amazing and unusual experience, full of uncertainty about what was to come. Camp Grant was the starting point of my military journey before heading to basic training.

After Camp Grant, I was sent to Camp Wheeler, Georgia, for thirteen weeks of basic infantry training. We learned rifle shooting, map reading, and field exercises under drill instructors. I lived in barracks there, close to many other trainees, preparing to be replacements for casualties overseas.

We left the United States from San Francisco to head overseas. It was my first time away from home for a long period, and it felt like an adventure. San Francisco was the embarkation point for my deployment to the Pacific Theater.

Our first stop overseas was New Caledonia, where they figured out which division we would join. It was a staging area before being assigned to the 31st Infantry Division. New Caledonia was a brief but important waypoint in my journey to combat.

I was assigned to the 31st Infantry Division, an Ohio National Guard unit that had been heavily engaged at Guadalcanal. We replaced casualties and trained further on Guadalcanal before moving on to combat at Bougainville. Serving with the 31st was intense and formative, especially during the long fight in the Solomon Islands.

The 31st Infantry Division I joined was part of the Ohio National Guard. Although I was from Chicago, I was assigned to this unit which had seen tough fighting in the Pacific. The Ohio National Guard division became my combat family during the war.

I spent about six or seven weeks on Guadalcanal after the main fighting was over, undergoing further training in the hills. The 31st Division was recovering from heavy losses there, and we prepared for future operations. Guadalcanal was a significant stop in my deployment, even though I arrived after the battle.

The 31st Infantry Division had been badly beaten in the Battle of Guadalcanal before I arrived. Although I did not fight in the battle myself, I was part of the division that replaced the casualties afterward. The battle's aftermath shaped the division's condition when I joined it.

Bougainville was my first combat experience, where we fought the Japanese for thirteen months. The fighting was intense and scary, especially the battle for Hill 700 where we lost several good friends. It was a dramatic and unforgettable part of my service in the Solomon Islands.

I served in the Solomon Islands campaign, including Guadalcanal and Bougainville. The islands were the setting for some of the toughest fighting I experienced, with long campaigns against the Japanese. The Solomon Islands were where I earned my Bronze Star and endured the hardships of jungle warfare.

I was awarded the Bronze Star Medal for my actions during the battle to recapture Hill 700 on Bougainville. Our whole platoon received the medal after crawling up the hill under heavy enemy fire. It was a proud but painful moment, as we lost several comrades in that fight.

Hill 700 on Bougainville was the site of our biggest and most intense battle. We crawled up the hill under enemy fire, losing four or five of my buddies along the way. Capturing that hill was a dramatic and unforgettable experience that earned us the Bronze Star Medal.

I remember Tom Brokaw's book where he called our generation the greatest generation, and I agree with him. Serving in the Army during World War II was a defining experience, and I felt proud to stand tall despite my small stature. His words resonate with my memories of sacrifice and service.

Seeing the casualties in the Iraq War hurts me deeply because I know what it's like to lose friends in combat. Having been through similar experiences in World War II, I empathize with the soldiers and their families. It reminds me of the sacrifices made during my own service in the Pacific.

After Bougainville, we moved on to the Philippine Islands, specifically Luzon, where we landed at Lingayen Gulf. We fought near Manila and Baguio, facing tough opposition in the Battle of Manila. The Philippines campaign was a major part of my service before I came down with malaria.

We invaded Luzon in the Philippines, landing at Lingayen Gulf with no initial opposition. The fighting intensified as we approached Manila, where our unit suffered casualties. Luzon was a critical battleground in the final phases of the Pacific War.

Our landing at Lingayen Gulf on Luzon was surprisingly unopposed, which was unexpected. From there, we moved inland toward Manila where the fighting became much fiercer. Lingayen Gulf was the gateway to the Philippines campaign for our unit.

The Battle of Manila was brutal, and our unit took heavy casualties, including the loss of our first lieutenant. I was fortunate to escape unharmed, but the fighting was intense and costly. That battle was one of the hardest parts of my service in the Philippines.

After the fierce fighting in Manila, we were sent to a rest area in a suburb of the city for several weeks. Manila was the site of some of the toughest combat I experienced, but also a place where we regrouped before moving on to Baguio. The city holds many memories of battle and recovery.

We were sent to Baguio, the summer capital of the Philippines located in the mountains, where we continued fighting the Japanese. It was a different environment from the lowlands, and after the fighting there, I came down with malaria. Baguio marked the final phase of my combat service.

I contracted malaria while stationed in Baguio, despite taking Atabrine daily to prevent it. I was hospitalized for several weeks but eventually recovered and rejoined my unit. Malaria was a harsh reminder of the tropical dangers we faced beyond combat.

We took Atabrine every day as a preventative measure against malaria while in the Philippines. Even with it, I still got sick, but I believe it helped lessen the severity. Atabrine was a constant part of our daily routine in the tropical environment.

I came down with malaria on August 6th, 1945, the same day the atomic bomb was dropped on Hiroshima. It was a strange coincidence that linked my personal suffering with a major historic event. That day remains etched in my memory alongside my illness.

I learned that the Library of Congress has a Veterans Memorial where you can enter names and stories of those who served. It’s comforting to know that our experiences and sacrifices are preserved there for future generations. I hope my story and those of my comrades live on in that archive.

The transcript cuts off before I could share much about the University of the Philippines, but during my time in the Philippines, I met many locals and formed friendships. The university is a significant institution in the country, and I likely encountered people connected to it during my service.
